posts.title: Rough Overview of SLAM
posts.text: This post is aimed for introducing basic SLAM knowledge, especially visual SLAM. Only the general pipeline will be list here, I won’t touch lots of algorithm details. This will be suitable for those who want to get rough overview of the SLAM. This post hasn’t been finished!!! Typing formulations is tring. For more details you can have a look at my SLAM presentation. In the future, I will write more posts about the SLAM components. What is SLAM Simultaneous localization and mapping(SLAM) consists in the concurrent construction of a model of the environment(the map), and the estimation of the state of the robot moving within it. – Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age To be easy, SLAM is a system which does Mapping(building a map) and Localization(locating the sensor) at the same time. Why we need SLAM First of all, we observe that the SLAM research done over the last decade has itself produced the visual-inertial odometry algorithm that currently represent the state-of-the-art. More generally, SLAM has directly led to the study of sensor fusion under more challenging setups (i.e., no GPS, low quality sensors) than previously considered in other literature (e.g., inertial navigation in aerospace engineering). The second answer regards the true topology of the environment. The third answer is that SLAM is needed for many applications that, either implicitly or explicitly, do require a globally consistent map. – Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age Types of SLAM CameraFor visual SLAM, there are three popular types of camera: Monocular Camera, Stereo Camera, RGB-D Camera. Although nowadays new types of camera have shown up, they havn’t been widely used. Monocular: Since monocular cameras are really cheap and easy to get, researchers pay lots of attention on the monocular SLAM. The main problem of Monocular SLAM is scale ambiguity. Stereo Camera: Use two cameras to recover the image depth. The Baseline(distance between two cameras) need to be known. When the baseline distance is larger, the stereo can see further. The main problem of stereo camera is difficult to calibration and high computation cost. RGB-D Camera: This camera type use structured light or ToF(Time-of-Flight) to get the depth map.Different from the stereo camera, RGB-D cameras don’t need to do lots of depth computation. The main problem of RGB-D camera is noisy data, limited field of view … RGB-D cameras are often used in indoor SLAM systems. For in-the-wild SLAM, it’s much more difficult to use RGB-D. Typical SLAM Pipeline The architecture of a SLAM system includes two main components: the front end and the back end. The front end abstracts sensor data into models that are amenable for estimation, while the back end performs inference on the abstracted data produced by the front end. – Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age The figure from paper shows the main components of SLAM pipeline. Though clear enough, it doesn’t explain enough details. Actually, I found people prefer to use following components to describe typical SLAM pipeline: Sensor Data Reading. Front End: Visual Odometry. Calculate camera motion between frames, build local map. Back End: Optimization. Accept camera pose of visual odometer, infomation of loop closing, then start optimization, to get global trajectory and map. Loop Closure. Detect loop. If exist, pass information to back end. Mapping. Build map. Visual OdometryVisual odeometry is the front-end of a SLAM system. It use adjacent images to estimate rough camera motion, and provide data for back-end. Basicly, there are two kinds of methods. Feature Based Methods. Feature based methods need to extract features from images first, and use those features to do computations. Features based methods are always viewed as the mainstream of visual odometry, because they are stable, and insensitive to light and motion. Direct Methods. Though feature is useful and robust, those methods suffer from the time consuming and feature losing. Direct methods could use all information in the images. Nowadays, direct methods are becoming important role in the SLAM area. Let’s have a close look at the feature based methods. Feature Extraction. Feature = key points + descriptor For SLAM, the feature should be robust, accurate and fast. ORB features are commonly used features. ORB features have scale and rotation invariance, and really fast. Epipolar Constraint. Epipolar constraint is basic geometry relationship in SLAM, and can be used to calculate camera motion. Triangulation. After using epipolar constrain t o get camera pose, we can use triangulation to fetch 3d points location. PnP Problem. When we already get 3D points locations, and we get 2D points, we can solve PnP problem to get camera locations. Point Cloud Matching: ICP. Back EndVisual odometry estimate short-term motion and structure. However, with the error accumulation, the map will gradully become unreliable. So, SLAM need to solve a long-term optimization problem to build large scale map. Extended Kalman Filter, EKF. With markov assumption, filter based methods like EKF use former state to predict next state. Bundle Adjustment. Current state is related to all the states before. Nonlinear optimization based methods like BA optimize all the states. Pose Graph. BA require high computaion. Pose graph give up landmard optimization, only optimize camera trajectory. Factor Graph. Loop ClosureLoop Closure add constraint for similar images, which helps to reduce error accumulation. The key is how to detect the loop. Bag-of-Words, BoW. BoW use word/dictionary to describe images. Using those words, we can calculate similarity between images. MappingReference Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age

posts.title: Structure From Motion
posts.text: The structure from motion problem in computer vision is the problem of recovering the 3D structure of a stationary scene from a set of projective measurements, represented as a collection of 2D images, via estimation of the motion of the camera corresponding to these images. Before Reading This post doesn’t contain any code. So if you want to understand SfM better, I highly recommend you to run SfM by yourself. Colmap is a good start point. Some are copied from structure from motion revisit. Please have a look at the original paper. BackgroundKnowing the 3D structure of real world is meaningful. It’s a generally scientific problem and core technology of a wide variety of fields, such as computer graphics, computer vision, virtual reality, digital media, etc. The structure from motion is one of the most important algorithms to recover 3D structure. It provides a useful process of reconstructing 3D structure from its projections into a series of images taken from different viewpoints. Problem Formulation Input: A collection of 2D images. output: 3D point cloud Assumptions: The scene is static ; … OverviewFor general SfM, it involves the three main stages: Extraction of features in images and matching these features between images. Camera motion estimation. Recovery of the 3D structure using the estimated motion and features. A variety of SfM strategies have been proposed including incremental, hierarchical and glbal approaches. Arguably, incremental SfM is the most popular strategy for reconstruction of unordered photo collections. Incremental SfM commonly starts with feature extraction and matching, followed by geometric verification. The resulting scene graph serves as the foundation for the reconstruction stage, which seeds the model with a carefully selected two-view reconstruction, before incrementally registering new images, triangulating scene points, filtering outliers, and refining the reconstruction using bundle adjustment (BA). Correspondence SearchFeature ExtractionWe wish to the features to be invariant under radiometric and geometric changes so that SfM can uniquely recognize them in multiple images. SIFT, its derivatives, and more recently learned features are the gold standard in terms of robustness. input: A set of images. output: $F_{i}={(x_{j},f_{j})|j=1…N}$ of local features at location $x_{j}\in R^{2}$ represented by an appearance descriptor $f_{j}$. MatchingSfM discovers images that see the same scene part by leveraging the features $F_{i}$ as an appearance description of the images. The naive approach tests every image pair for scene overlap; it searches for feature correspondences by finding the most similar feature in image $I_{a}$ for every feature in image $I_{b}$, using a similarity metric comparing the appearance $f_{j}$ of the features. However, this approach has computational complexity $O(N^{2})$ and is prohibitive for large image collections. A variety of approaches tackle the problem of scalable and efficient matching. input：$F_{i}={(x_{j},f_{j})|j=1…N}$ output: A set of potentially overlapping image pairs $C={I_{a},I_{b}|I_{a},I_{b}\in I, a < b }$ and their associated feature correspondences $M_{ab}\in F_{a}\times F_{b}$. Geometric VerificationSince matching is based solely on appearance, it is not guaranteed that corresponding features actually map to the same scene point. Therefore, SfM verifies the matches by trying to estimate a transformation that maps feature points between images using projective geometry. A homography H describes the transformation of a purely rotating or a moving camera capturing a planar scene. Epipolar geometry describes the relation for a moving camera through the essential matrix E (calibrated) or the fundamental matrix F (uncalibrated), and can be extended to three views using the trifocal tensor. If a valid transformation maps a sufficient number of features between the images, they are considered geometrically verified. input: A set of potentially overlapping image pairs $ C={I_{a},I_{b}|I_{a},I_{b}\in I, a < b }$and their associated feature correspondences $M_{ab}\in F_{a}\times F_{b}$. output: Geometrically verified image pairs $C={I_{a},I_{b}|I_{a},I_{b}\in I, a < b } $and their associated feature correspondences $M_{ab}\in F_{a}\times F_{b}$. The output of this stage is a so-called scene graph with images as nodes and verified pairs of images as edges. Incremental ReconstructionInitializationSfM initializes the model with a carefully selected two-view reconstruction. Choosing a suitable initial pair is critical, since the reconstruction may never recover from a bad initialization. Image RegistrationStarting from a metric reconstruction, new images can be registered to the current model by solving the Perspective-n-Point (PnP) problem using feature correspondences to triangulated points in already registered images. The PnP problem involves estimating the pose $P_{c}$ and, for uncalibrated cameras, its intrinsic parameters. The set $P$ is thus extended by the pose $P_{c}$ of the newly registered image. TriangulationA newly registered image must observe existing scene points. In addition, it may also increase scene coverage by extending the set of points X through triangulation. A new scene point $X_{k}$ can be triangulated and added to $X$ as soon as at least one more image, also covering the new scene part but from a different viewpoint, is registered. Triangulation is a crucial step in SfM, as it increases the stability of the existing model through redundancy and it enables registration of new images by providing additional 2D-3D correspondences. Bundle AdjustmentImage registration and triangulation are separate procedures, even though their products are highly correlated uncertainties in the camera pose propagate to triangulated points and vice versa, and additional triangulations may improve the initial camera pose through increased redundancy. Without further refinement, SfM usually drifts quickly to a non-recoverable state. BA is the joint non-linear refinement of camera parameters $P_{c}$ and point parameters $X_{k}$ that minimizes the reprojection error$$E=\Sigma_{j}\rho_{j}(||\pi(P_{c},X_{k})-x_{j}||^{2}_{2})$$ using a function $\pi$ that projects scene points to image space and a loss function $\rho_{j}$ to potentially down-weight outliers. Reference structure from motion revisit A survey of structure from motion

posts.title: Hello World
posts.text: Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub. Quick StartCreate a new post1$ hexo new "My New Post" More info: Writing Run server1$ hexo server More info: Server Generate static files1$ hexo generate More info: Generating Deploy to remote sites1$ hexo deploy More info: Deployment

posts.title: DensePose
posts.text: Human pose estimation has been a popular research topic in the past few years. Machines are able to recover human skeleton structure by estimating 3d human key points. However, key points are one kind of sparse representations. Sometimes we want a more dense representation. This paper gives a possible solution towards predicting dense correspondences from an image to a surface-based model. Overview Gather dense correspondences for 50K persons appearing in the COCO dataset by introducing an effieient annotation pipeline. Use this dataset to train CNN-based systems that deliver dense correspondences ‘in the wild’, by regressing body surface coordinates at any image pixel. Use sparse correspondences to train a ‘teacher’ network that can ‘inpaint’ the supervision signal in the rest of the image domain. This gain better performance when compared to either sparse points or any other existing dataset. DATASET Called COCO-Dense Pose. UV map defination: Head, hands, feet: SMPL defined UV map Other parts: obtain the unwrapping via multidimensional scaling applied to pairwise geodesic distances. Annotation: 1. Region; 2. Sparse correspondences NetworkRegion-based dense pose regression Multitask cascaded architectures Learning Even though the target is dense pose estimation at test time, in every training sample, the dataset only annotate a sparse subset of the pixels, approximately 100-150 per human. This is not a problem during training, the solution is to making classification/regression losses oblivious to points where the group-truth correspondence was not collected, simply but not including them in the summation over the per-pixel losses. By ‘inpainting’ the values of the supervision signal on positions that were not originally annotated, the network botain better results. For this, the author adopts a learning-based approach where they firstly train a ‘teacher’ network to reconstruct the ground-truth values wherever these are observed, and then deploy it on the full image domain, yielding a dense supervision signal. Result

posts.title: Hand and Object Pose Estimation and Reconstruction
posts.text: In 2019, there are two new papers introducing hand-object interaction, one paper (H+O: Unified Egocentric Recognition of 3D Hand-Object Poses and Interactions) estiamtes poses of hand and object, and uses hand-object relationship to do high-level tasks like action recognition. Another paper (Learning joint reconstruction of hands and manipulated objects) does jointly shape reconstruction for hand and object, and use relationships between them to help realistic reconstruction. This post give a simple introduction towards those two papers, and give some thinkings. When we talk about Hand-Object InteractionBefore we start introducing these two paper, we should make some defination for hand-object interaction, and understand why we need it, why it is important for both researcher and engineer. When we talk about hand-object interaction, we are saying tasks which contains more information than hand-only or object-only tasks. If we are handing hands and object at the same time, there are real word constrains and high-level releationships like action between hands and object. Generally, hand-object interaction is one kind of relationship between human hands and object, including geometry constrains like contact and recognition relationship like action purpose. Why we need to model this kind of relationship? I think it benifits both recognition and construction tasks. Here I copy part of the papers: Human behavior can be characterized by the individual actions a person takes in interaction with the surrounding objects and the environment. Accurate estimation of human hands, as well as their interactions with the physical world, is vital to better understand human actions and interactions. In particular, recovering the 3D shape of a hand is key to many applications including virtual and augmented reality, human-computer interaction, action recognition and imitation-based learning of robotic skills. PipelinesJointly Hand-Object Pose Estimation H+O: Unified Egocentric Recognition of 3D Hand-Object Poses and Interactions, CVPR19 oral Author: Bugra Tekin, who is working on human pose estimation, object pose estimation. Microsoft Dataset: FPHA-HO. Subset of FPHA. Action recognition dataset. Contains annotations for 6D object pose and hand pose. 4 objects. 10 different action categories. EgoDexter: Real dataset. 3D hand pose nad 6D object pose. Add annotation by hands. For evaluation. SynthHands: Synthetic hands pose estimation dataset. Pipeline: We propose a unified framework for recognizing 3D hand and object interactions by simultaneously solving four tasks in a feed-forward pass through a neural network: 3D hand pose estimation, object pose estimation, object recognition and activity classification. Our method operates on monocular color images and relies on joint features that are shared among all the tasks. Hand-Object Interaction Modeling: Our scheme relies on a common output representation for both hands and objects that parametrizes their pose with 3D control points. Jointly Hand-Object Reconstruction Learning joint reconstruction of hands and manipulated objects, CVPR19 Author: Yana Hasson, phd student INRIA. Her first paper. Interestingly, I find I ran her I3D code before. Dataset: FPHA-HO. Subset of FPHA. Action recognition dataset. Contains annotations for 6D object pose and hand pose. 4 objects. 10 different action categories. HIC. Hand and object meshes are provided. ObMan: Synthetic. To overcome the lack of adequate training data. Pipeline: Our network architecture is split into two branches. The first branch reconstructs the object shape in a normalized coordinate space. The second branch predicts the hand mesh as well as the information necessary to transfer the object to the hand-relative coordinate system. Each branch has a ResNet18 encoder pre-trained on ImageNet. Hand-Object Interaction Modeling: prior knowledge that objects can not interpenetrate each other and that, when grasping objects, contacts occur at the surface between the object and the hand. Repulsion: penalizes hand and object interpenetration. Attraction: penalize cases in which hand vertices are in the vicinity of the object but the surfaces are not in contact. What’s the next step?I believe 2019 is the start year for jointly hand-object interaction learning from RGB data. Before these two papers, research on hand-object interaction are rely on RGB-D data. I haven’t read those papers yet, so I won’t compare them here. Let’s have a close look at these two papers. For H+O, its h-o modeling is rough. But since its final task is action recognition, some loss on geometry space is acceptable. Using the same presentation, the network can predict hand key points and object keypoints at the same time, and finally get hand pose and object pose by solving a PnP problem. This method doesn’t explicitly model the interaction between hand and object, but let network to learn the relationship. To be honest, this seems a little too simple. I am not quite sure if a more accurate modeling will help recognition tasks. Learning joint reconstruction of hands and manipulated objects uses and end-to-end pipeline to do hand-object reconstruction jointly. Make interaction as a loss item is a simple but effect idea. I believe this simple pipeline will be an important reference for next year works. For the future trends, possible ideas are as follows: Third-Person View. Nowadays hand-object are mainly working on first-person images. This is mainly because the lack of third-person view data. But we need third-person view to help understand hand-object interaction. Those will definitely help third-person view human reconstruction. Add Semantic Prior. Add semantic prior will help Han-object reconstruction. Since those semantic information gives a rough idea about the relation of hand and object. More Accurate Interaction Modeling. Contact is the main interaction form. So there are questions: Is contact the only interaction relationship between hand and object? How to model the contact? Are repulsion and attraction enough to describe contact? Thanks.
